name: Things 3
strengths: Local data by default — no cloud account required for Mac
Excellent UX with thoughtful interaction design
Natural language date input and repeating tasks
weaknesses: $49.99 upfront cost for the Mac app alone
No Windows version, no web app
Limited keyboard shortcuts — designed for mouse and trackpad
No command palette for quick actions
Custom views are limited compared to split views with filter rules
No open data format — you can't script or query your task database
best_for: Mac users who value a beautiful, polished experience and are happy to pay once for well-crafted software
not_for: Users who need extensive keyboard shortcuts, free software, or the ability to query and script their task data
switch_reasons: Don't want to spend $49.99 on a task manager
Want Vim-style keyboard navigation and a command palette
Need custom filtered views (split views) beyond Things 3's Areas/Projects structure
Want to script or query your task data with SQL
vs_intro: Things 3 and SuperTasks are both local-first Mac task managers — and that's where the similarities end. Things 3 is a premium, beautifully crafted app that costs $49.99. SuperTasks is free, built around keyboard speed, and gives you direct access to your data. If you're choosing between them, the core question is: do you want a polished experience you pay for once, or a keyboard-native tool that costs nothing?
alternatives_intro: People look for Things 3 alternatives for a few reasons: the upfront $49.99 cost is a barrier, the keyboard shortcuts don't go deep enough for power users, or they need custom filtered views that go beyond Areas and Projects. Here are the best alternatives worth considering.

other_alternatives.name: OmniFocus
other_alternatives.note: The most powerful Mac task manager for GTD practitioners. Custom perspectives, AppleScript support, local data. Significantly more expensive and complex than Things 3.

other_alternatives.name: Todoist
other_alternatives.note: Cross-platform with a lower barrier to entry. Cloud-synced and requires a subscription for full features. Better mobile app than Things 3, but loses the local-data advantage.

other_alternatives.name: Notion
other_alternatives.note: Flexible workspace that can be configured as a task manager. Much more setup required, and far heavier than a dedicated task app. Better for project management than daily task capture.

faq.q: Is SuperTasks a good free alternative to Things 3?
faq.a: SuperTasks is the strongest free alternative to Things 3 on Mac. Both store data locally and work offline. The difference: SuperTasks has 22+ keyboard shortcuts and a command palette where Things 3 is more mouse-oriented, and SuperTasks costs nothing where Things 3 is $49.99 upfront.

faq.q: Does SuperTasks have a command palette like Things 3?
faq.a: Yes — SuperTasks has a full command palette (⌘K) that reaches every view, task, and action in the app. Things 3 has Quick Find for searching but lacks a command palette for firing actions without a mouse.

faq.q: How does SuperTasks compare to Things 3 for keyboard users?
faq.a: SuperTasks is purpose-built for keyboard navigation. It has Vim-style j/k movement, g-chord view switching (G+I for Inbox, G+T for Today, etc.), single-key task actions (C to create, D to complete, R for due date), and ⌘K for everything else. Things 3 has some keyboard shortcuts but is fundamentally a trackpad-first app.

faq.q: Can you access your task data in SuperTasks like Things 3?
faq.a: SuperTasks stores all data in a local SQLite file at ~/Library/Application Support/SuperTasks/tasks.db — you can open it with any SQLite browser, write SQL queries, or build scripts on top of it. Things 3 uses a proprietary database format that isn't directly accessible.
